mainEntity.name: Can I shoot my Airbnb listing on a phone?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — a modern smartphone on a tripod produces perfectly acceptable Airbnb photography for most listings. The tripod matters more than the camera. Avoid the phone's ultra-wide setting, which distorts corners.

mainEntity.name: What's the best time of day to photograph my holiday let?

acceptedAnswer.text: Bright-overcast days are easiest in the UK because light is soft and windows aren't blown out. On sunny days, shoot each room when the sun is off its windows.

mainEntity.name: How many photos should I put in my Airbnb listing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Thirty to forty photos is the sweet spot. Cover every room from two angles, bathrooms, exterior, outdoor space, and any unique features.

mainEntity.name: Should I use HDR for Airbnb photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: No — heavy HDR signals estate agent photos and exaggeration. A very light HDR effect in a single frame is fine, but avoid fully processed HDR composites.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a wide-angle lens?

acceptedAnswer.text: A wide-angle lens helps with small UK rooms but stay in the 16–24mm range on full frame (10–16mm on APS-C). Wider creates visible distortion that looks misleading.

mainEntity.name: How high should the camera be when shooting an Airbnb listing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Chair height — around 1.2 to 1.4 metres. Eye-height shots look down into rooms and make ceilings feel low. Chair height gives equal weight to floor and ceiling.

mainEntity.name: Can I photograph my Airbnb with guests staying there?

acceptedAnswer.text: Only if the listing is empty of personal belongings. Photograph between bookings, during a deep clean, or on a dedicated changeover day.

mainEntity.name: When is it worth hiring a professional instead?

acceptedAnswer.text: When you've recently refurbished, when competing in a crowded market, or when running multiple properties. A professional shoot often pays back inside one season.
